humanities_role_explanation: MILU evaluates general-purpose models on regional cultural knowledge (content) and contains dedicated subjects like Arts & Culture, History, Literature, and Music & Performing Arts (domain).
benchmark_modalities_explanation: The benchmark consists entirely of text-only multiple-choice questions (MCQs) in 11 Indic languages and English.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Models are evaluated on factual recognition and reasoning related to regional history, arts, festivals, grammar, and laws of India.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Evaluates a wide range of subjects (41 subjects across 8 domains) including STEM, Law, Business, and Humanities.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Benchmarked over 42 LLMs including GPT-4o, Gemini, Llama-3, Gemma, and various language-specific Indic models.

llm_is_primary_system.note: Generative LLMs are the primary systems being benchmarked.

arts_humanities_data.note: Includes subjects in History, Literature and Linguistics, Religious Studies, and Arts and Culture (Visual Arts and Performing Arts).

quantitative_metrics.note: Uses accuracy (multiple-choice question selection accuracy) calculated via conditional log-likelihood and generative parsing.

published_after_march_2023.note: First published as a preprint in November 2024.

english_language.note: The scientific paper is written in English, and English is also one of the evaluated languages in the benchmark.

summary: This paper introduces MILU (Multi-task Indic Language Understanding), a comprehensive multi-task benchmark spanning 41 subjects across 11 Indic languages. Sourced from competitive national and state-level exams, MILU evaluates both general knowledge and India-specific cultural nuances. The authors perform an extensive evaluation of 42 open, closed, and language-specific models, revealing substantial gaps in their multilingual and cultural performance.
key_findings: GPT-4o achieved the highest average accuracy at 74.74%, followed by other closed models. Open-source multilingual models significantly outperformed domain-specific Indic models, which often performed near the random baseline (25%). Furthermore, models performed notably worse on culturally relevant domains like Arts & Humanities, Social Sciences, and Law & Governance compared to general STEM subjects, suggesting a critical lack of local cultural data in training corpora.
